---
title: "Supported Input"
---

This page discusses the fact-patterns of input the `state-estimation` app supports. Because everything on this page has been verified for the inputs `state-estimation` accepts, this page effectively replaces what would be in-code data verification layers for an app that runs on arbitrary data.

`state-estimation` is built to run on the `scene-XXXX_pose.json` and `scene-XXXX_ms_imu.json` files from the nuScenes CAN bus expansion published February 2020 by nuScenes.[@nuscenes-canbus-readme; @nuscenes-canbus-api; @nuscenes-canbus-local-2026-03-26]

`state-estimation` also runs on the `scene_pose.json` and `scene_ms_imu.json` files bundled with the app. These files were built with a simulation to follow all of the input fact-patterns the app supports.

## Allowed input layouts

- `can_bus` lives under the nuScenes root, e.g. `/data/sets/nuscenes/can_bus`, and is documented in `python-sdk/nuscenes/can_bus/README.md`. The CAN-bus API loads per-scene files as `scene-0001_<message>.json`; in the local corpus they are hyphenated, e.g. `scene-0001_ms_imu.json` and `scene-0001_pose.json`. The local corpus contains `979` `scene-*_ms_imu.json` files and `979` `scene-*_pose.json` files across sparse scene ids from `scene-0001` through `scene-1110`; the largest scene id is not the scene count. It includes neither `scene_gps.json` nor bundled-style files like `scene_pose.json` or `scene_ms_imu.json`. The local `can_bus` directory also contains non-schema audit artifacts such as `audit.py`, `audit_out.txt`, `file_list.txt`, `pos_z_audit.json`, and `pos_z_audit_summary.json`. [@nuscenes-devkit-root-readme; @nuscenes-canbus-readme; @nuscenes-canbus-api; @nuscenes-canbus-local-2026-03-26]

- Allowed pose files always yield at least two pose-derived GPS samples with positive $dt$ for initialization. Allowed runs always have a non-empty IMU stream and pose truth for every GPS timestamp used by GPS updates. In `110 / 979` allowed nuScenes pairs, one final pose sample occurs after the last IMU sample; the largest gap is `31,368 us`. That single trailing pose entry is treated as noise and ignored. Since the GPS stream is simulated using the pose samples, ignoring the single trailing pose avoids one last GPS measurement after the IMU stream ends. In turn, we can guarantee the IMU stream covers the full generated GPS stream for all `979` allowed input pairs.

- The pose file is the project's reference trajectory for truth lookup, checks, and evaluation. [@nuscenes-canbus-readme; @caesar2020nuscenes; @nuscenes-issue-1111]


## Timestamps

- each file is a JSON array [@nuscenes-canbus-local-2026-03-26]
- timestamps are integer microseconds [@nuscenes-canbus-readme]
- the streams refer to the same scene and the same time axis

## IMU orientation input

- `q` is interpreted as ${}^{A}q_{\fsub{I}}$, mapping IMU coordinates into a gravity-aligned frame with arbitrary yaw [@nuscenes-canbus-readme]
- ${}^{A}q_{\fsub{I}}$ is useful for tilt [@nuscenes-canbus-readme]
- map yaw comes from GPS motion during initialization

## Stream roles and provenance

- Table 2 lists a `GPS`/`IMU`/`AHRS` package with `0.2` degree heading accuracy, `0.1` degree roll/pitch accuracy, `20 mm` RTK positioning, and `1000 Hz` update rate. nuScenes provides raw CAN-bus data including velocities, accelerations, torque, steering angles, and wheel speeds, and localizes the vehicle by building a lidar HD map offline and then running online Monte Carlo Localization with lidar and odometry; reported localization error is `<= 10 cm`. The CAN-bus README describes the CAN-bus data as highly experimental. [@caesar2020nuscenes; @nuscenes-canbus-readme]

- Pose `pos` matches higher-rate `ego_pose`; correspondingly, `scene-XXXX_pose.json` is treated as near-ground-truth pose, consistent with maintainer clarification in issue `#1111` and the reported `<= 10 cm` lidar-map-based vehicle localization. [@nuscenes-canbus-readme; @nuscenes-issue-1111; @caesar2020nuscenes]

## Frames and coordinate conventions

- The dataset uses an ego frame for calibration and frame relationships, with sensor extrinsics relative to the midpoint of the rear axle. In this project, ego/body and IMU are treated as the same Forward-Left-Up frame there; nuScenes staff say the IMU reference uses `x` forward, `y` left, `z` up. Pose `pos` is in the global frame; in this project, global frame `G` is treated as a map-local Cartesian frame in meters with reference `(0, 0, 0)` at the map top-left, `x` and `y` in the ground plane, and `z` up. Pose `accel`, `rotation_rate`, and `vel` are in the ego-vehicle frame. `schema_nuscenes.md` says `ego_pose` localization is 2D in the `xy` plane and `ego_pose.translation[2]` is always `0`; `schema_nuimages.md` says ego-pose acceleration `z` is close to gravitational acceleration $g = 9.81\ \mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}^2$. [@caesar2020nuscenes; @nuscenes-canbus-readme; @nuscenes-issue-108; @nuscenes-issue-56; @nuscenes-issue-699; @nuscenes-issue-1066; @nuscenes-issue-122; @nuscenes-schema; @nuimages-schema]

- The CAN-bus README does not state an order explicitly for `q` or `pose.orientation`, so `w, x, y, z` is inferred from the broader schema convention. The README describes pose `orientation` as a 4-element "rotation vector in the ego vehicle frame"; given the `ego_pose` identity statement, the broader schema convention, and the downloaded unit-norm data, treat it as a quaternion-like rotation field, not a literal 3D rotation vector. [@nuscenes-schema; @nuimages-schema; @nuscenes-canbus-readme; @nuscenes-canbus-local-2026-03-26]

## File schema and timestamps

- Across all `979` `ms_imu` files, every record uses the exact key set `linear_accel`, `q`, `rotation_rate`, `utime`, with vector lengths `3, 4, 3` for `linear_accel`, `q`, and `rotation_rate`. Across all `979` `pose` files, every record uses the exact key set `accel`, `orientation`, `pos`, `rotation_rate`, `utime`, `vel`, with vector lengths `3, 4, 3, 3, 3` for `accel`, `orientation`, `pos`, `rotation_rate`, and `vel`. All `979` `ms_imu` files and all `979` `pose` files have strictly increasing `utime`. [@nuscenes-canbus-readme; @nuscenes-canbus-local-2026-03-26]

## Cadence and coverage statistics

- Nominal rates are `100 Hz` for `ms_imu` and `50 Hz` for `pose`. For the streams this app uses, per-scene counts stay in tight bands, and `len(ms_imu) / len(pose)` ranges from about `1.982` to `2.025` with median about `2.002`: `pose` min `780`, median `973`, mean `972.526`, max `996`; `ms_imu` min `1565`, median `1947`, mean `1947.859`, max `1994`. The local corpus does not live on a perfect integer-clock grid: median per-file sample intervals are `20008 us` for `pose` and `9993 us` for `ms_imu`. There is real timestamp jitter and occasional larger gaps: observed global $dt$ ranges are `3280 us` to `80617 us` for `pose` and `1006 us` to `50001 us` for `ms_imu`. [@nuscenes-canbus-readme; @nuscenes-canbus-local-2026-03-26]

## Empirical signal structure

- Across `952,103` checked pose records, `pose` has exact planar form `pos = [x, y, 0]`, `orientation = [w, 0, 0, z]`, `vel = [forward, 0, 0]`; pose quaternions are unit length to machine precision. Despite yaw-only pose orientation, ego-frame dynamics are nontrivial: `pose.accel[2] mean/med/min/max = 9.784/9.789/4.818/15.063`; `|pose.rotation_rate[0]|, |pose.rotation_rate[1]|, |pose.rotation_rate[2]| med/max = (0.0074, 0.0079, 0.0086)/(0.3223, 0.4490, 0.6947)` $\mathrm{rad}/\mathrm{s}$. [@nuscenes-canbus-local-2026-03-26]

- In `ms_imu`, `linear_accel` and `rotation_rate` are expressed in the IMU frame in $\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}^2$ and $\mathrm{rad}/\mathrm{s}$. After rotating `linear_accel` into the gravity-aligned frame defined by `q` and restricting to near-stationary moments, samples cluster near `[0, 0, 9.8]`, not `[0, 0, 0]` (`147006` matched samples; stationary mean `[0.00016, 0.00224, 9.79705]`; stationary median `[-0.00007, 0.00126, 9.79619]`). IMU `q` is essentially unit length, with max observed norm error around `1.36e-7`, but not pure yaw: `|q[1]|, |q[2]| med/mean/max = (0.0071, 0.0080)/(0.0098, 0.0101)/(0.0712, 0.0701)`. `linear_accel` is full 3D: `|linear_accel[0]|, |linear_accel[1]| med/mean/max = (0.389, 0.241)/(0.522, 0.349)/(5.491, 4.541)`; `linear_accel[2] mean/med/min/max = 9.784/9.786/-2.802/21.610`. [@nuscenes-canbus-readme; @nuscenes-canbus-linear-accel-specific-force-local-2026-03-26; @nuscenes-canbus-local-2026-03-26]

## File-name variation

- The CAN-bus README prose sometimes uses `scene_0001_*` and `scene_0001_imu.json`, but the API code and examples use `scene-0001_*` and `scene-0001_ms_imu.json`. [@nuscenes-canbus-readme; @nuscenes-canbus-api]
